[REASONING CONTEXT]
Amplify: prior_distribution_weighting; posterior_update_cycle; evidential_accumulation
Suppress: letting_initial_probability_estimate_persist_unchanged_despite_new_contradicting_data; ignoring_population_level_frequency_data_when_evaluating_individual_case_likelihood
[END REASONING CONTEXT]

The friend's error is naively adding probabilities. The correct approach uses the **complement rule**:

P(at least one day of rain) = 1 − P(no rain on all three days)

- P(no rain on a single day) = 1 − 0.30 = 0.70
- P(no rain on all three days) = 0.70 × 0.70 × 0.70 = 0.343
- P(at least one day of rain) = 1 − 0.343 = **0.657 = 65.7%**

The answer is **(A) 65.7%**.

The friend's "30% × 3 = 90%" fails because you can't simply add probabilities of independent events — that method double-counts overlapping outcomes (e.g., it raining on day 1 *and* day 2). The complement approach correctly accounts for all combinations.
